title: Unit 06 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Reflection, Presentation, and Professional Growth.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of reflection in career development?

choices.text: To avoid reviewing past work
choices.feedback: Reflection requires looking back at work and progress, not avoiding it.

choices.text: To remove the need for goals
choices.feedback: Reflection can support goal setting and revision rather than replace it.

choices.text: To focus only on mistakes with no strengths
choices.feedback: Strong reflection includes both strengths and areas for improvement.

choices.text: To think about growth, strengths, and areas for improvement
choices.feedback: Correct. Reflection helps students review their progress and identify both strengths and next steps.

questions.prompt: Feedback can be used to improve portfolio items or other work.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Feedback is useful because it can help students revise and strengthen their work.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Feedback can help identify what should be revised or improved.

choices.text: Submitting a weak first draft without changes
choices.feedback: Revision involves making improvements, not leaving work unchanged.

choices.text: Ignoring comments from a teacher or reviewer
choices.feedback: Revision often begins by using feedback from others.

choices.text: Improving wording, organization, or detail after review
choices.feedback: Correct. Revision means making a piece of work stronger after reviewing it.

choices.text: Replacing every item without checking quality
choices.feedback: Revision should be purposeful and focused on quality.

questions.prompt: Which presentation habit is most professional?

choices.text: Reading every word in a rushed voice
choices.feedback: A rushed reading style usually makes a presentation harder to follow.

choices.text: Speaking clearly and organizing ideas in a logical order
choices.feedback: Correct. Clear delivery and logical organization are important presentation skills.

choices.text: Skipping the main point of the presentation
choices.feedback: A strong presentation should make the main point clear.

choices.text: Using no preparation at all
choices.feedback: Preparation usually improves clarity and confidence.

questions.prompt: A portfolio presentation should explain why selected artifacts matter.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. A strong portfolio presentation explains what the artifacts are and why they are important.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Artifacts should be explained so the audience understands their purpose.

questions.prompt: What should a student do before giving a portfolio presentation?

choices.text: Avoid planning the order of ideas
choices.feedback: Planning helps a presentation stay clear and organized.

choices.text: Show every item without deciding what matters most
choices.feedback: A strong presentation is selective and focused.

choices.text: Choose a few strong artifacts and prepare how to explain them
choices.feedback: Correct. Preparation should include selecting strong evidence and planning how to present it.

choices.text: Rely only on guessing during the presentation
choices.feedback: Preparation usually leads to stronger delivery and clearer explanation.

questions.prompt: A student says, "I improved because my newer portfolio items are clearer and better organized." What skill is the student showing?

choices.text: Reflection
choices.feedback: Correct. The student is reflecting on growth by identifying changes and evidence of improvement.

choices.text: Avoiding revision
choices.feedback: The statement shows awareness of improvement, not avoidance of revision.

choices.text: Ignoring presentation skills
choices.feedback: The statement is about reflection on growth, not ignoring presentation.

choices.text: Choosing random artifacts
choices.feedback: The statement focuses on improvement, not random selection.

questions.prompt: Why is organization important in a portfolio presentation?

choices.text: Because it makes the presentation harder to follow
choices.feedback: Organization should improve understanding, not make it harder.

choices.text: Because it removes the need to explain artifacts
choices.feedback: Artifacts still need explanation even when the presentation is organized well.

choices.text: Because it means every slide or item should be read word for word
choices.feedback: Organization helps structure ideas, but it does not mean reading everything word for word.

choices.text: Because it helps the audience understand the order and purpose of the content
choices.feedback: Correct. Clear organization helps the audience follow the presentation and understand the message.
